item.title: Appwrite 社区报告 #10 | Appwrite Community Report #10
item.description:
用于Appwrite的haimantika mitra

发表于来自 Appwrite 团队的您好！👋

你这一周过得怎么样？ 对我们来说，随着我们致力于更多的集成和功能，它非常有成效！ 我们正在进行一些赠品，添加了一些功能，社区贡献，资源等等！

立即参与赢取 Appwrite 连帽衫🤩
我们刚刚超过 90K 的追随者！ 您是维护者还是认识维护者？ 告诉他们OSS 基金- 为期一年的支持开源项目维护者的基金。

这是为了让 Appwrite 每天都变得更好！ 本周我们处理的问题：

  • 社区成员Bishwajeet Parhi添加了 DailyMotion OAuth Provider
    将整个 Appwrite 堆栈升级到 Docker Compose V2 的进展
    现在可以将文件从 n8n 上传到 Appwrite 存储
    努力增加查询的最大大小。

  • 改进哈希 API
  • 添加 Vimeo 作为 OAuth 提供者
  • 审查 OSS 基金申请，接近宣布我们赞助的第一个维护者

社区成员Maxime Barthomeuf建议添加 Puppeteer 支持，您怎么看？
只需一个命令即可在 Appwrite 中启动云功能。
  • 了解如何在 Appwrite 中管理项目
  • 接受 30 天的学习挑战，开始使用 Appwrite 。

    • 洛伦佐·扎兰托内洛 - Jun 17 6月17日



    原文:

    haimantika mitra for Appwrite

    Posted on Jun 17

    Hello from team Appwrite!👋

    How has been your week? For us, it has been quite productive as we work towards more integrations and features! 👀

    TLDR; We have some giveaways ongoing, some features added, community contributions, resources and more!

    📢 What’s new

    • Appwrite just crossed 90,000 followers on Twitter and we are doing a giveaway! Participate now to win Appwrite hoodies🤩
      WE JUST CROSSED 90K FOLLOWERS! 🤩Celebrate this milestone with us as we race towards 100K followers with an awesome swag giveaway ⚡1️⃣ Like + RT this tweet

      2️⃣ Follow @appwrite

      3️⃣ Share what feature you're looking forward to in Appwrite v1.0

      4️⃣ Win 1 out of 5 Appwrite hoodies

    • Are you a maintainer or know a maintainer? Tell them about OSS Fund - a year long fund to support open-source project maintainers.

    ✅ Issues solved

    Here’s to making Appwrite better everyday! Issues we have worked on this week:

    • GitLab OAuth now allows self hosted instances
      🔗Link to PR

    • Addition of DailyMotion OAuth Provider by community member Bishwajeet Parhi
      🔗Link to PR

    • Advancements towards upgrading the entire Appwrite stack to Docker Compose V2
      🔗Link to PR

    • Files can now be uploaded from n8n to Appwrite Storage
      🔗Link to PR

    ⚒️ What we’re currently working on

    • Working to increase the maximum size of a query.
    • Improving Hashing API
    • Adding Vimeo as OAuth provider
    • Reviewing OSS Fund applications, getting closer to announcing the first maintainer we sponsor

    🗣 Discussions

    Community member Maxime Barthomeuf, suggested adding Puppeteer support, what do you think about it?
    🔗 Join the discussion here

    📘 Resources

    • Initiate a cloud function in Appwrite with just one command.
    • Learn about managing project in Appwrite
    • Take up the 30 days learning challenge to get started with Appwrite.

    Bervianto Leo Pratama - Jun 17

    Lorenzo Zarantonello - Jun 17
item.author: haimantika

item.title: 在反应中使用钩子调用您的 API 🧑🏽‍💻 | Calling your APIs with hooks in react 🧑🏽‍💻
item.description:
大家好！ 这是我的第一篇文章，我想带来一个有趣的话题：

  • 我们如何从我们的 react 项目中调用 API？
  • 最好的方法是什么？

当然，没有灵丹妙药，这取决于您正在从事的项目。 今天我将分享一些您可以在通话中进行的优化，这些优化可能会引发新的想法。

问题

在我的职业生涯中，我从事过不同的项目，我发现了这样的事情：

示例 1

 export const MyComponent : React . FC = () => { const [ dogs , setDogs ] = useState (); useEffect (() => { fetch ( ' /api/v1/dogs ' ) . then ( r => r . json ()) . then ( json => setData ( json )); }); return < DogsList dogs = { dogs } />; } export const MyComponent2 : React . FC = () => { const [ cats , setCats ] = useState (); useEffect (() => { fetch ( ' /api/v1/cats ' ) . then ( r => r . json ()) . then ( json => setData ( json )); }); return < CatsList cats = { cats } />; }

或这个：

示例 2

 const MyComponent : React . FC = () => { const [ loading , setLoading ] = useState ( true ); const [ error , setError ] = useState (); const [ dogs , setDogs ] = useState (); useEffect (() => { fetch ( ' /api/v1/dogs ' ) . then ( r => r . json ()) . then ( json => setDogs ( json )) . then (() => setLoading ( false )) . catch ( e => setError ( e )); }); if ( loading ) { return < div > Loading dogs </ div >; } return < DogsList dogs = { dogs } />; }

如您所见，代码开始重复，我们将通信逻辑放入组件中。 如果我们想添加更多逻辑，那就更糟了，即仅在组件已挂载时才设置状态。

出于某种原因，人们有时会忘记我们可以创建一个简单的钩子来处理所有这些场景并保持我们的代码更干净。

注意：当然，有一些库可以解决这个问题，但出于本文的目的，我更喜欢我们自己实现它

让我们从一个从 API 检索数据的新钩子的小实现开始。 由于我们擅长在这里命名，我们称之为useApi ：

 function useApi ( url : string ) { const [ data , setData ] = useState (); useEffect (() => { fetch ( url ) . then ( r => r . json ()) . then ( json => setData ( json )) }, [ url ]) // Remember your dependencies return data ; }

只有使用这个简单的钩子，我们才能将第一个示例重写为：

 export const MyComponent : React . FC = () => { const dogs = useApi ( ' /api/v1/dogs ' ); return < DogsList dogs = { dogs } />; } export const MyComponent2 : React . FC = () => { const cats = useApi ( ' /api/v1/cats ' ); return < CatsList cats = { cats } />; }

看看这有多干净，我的组件不关心我们如何调用这个 API，如果我们使用fetch或axios ，我们只知道数据会在那里。

进步的一小步

让我们再重复一下，我们忘记了我们在这里拥有的一些力量......我们有打字稿！ 而且我们没有使用它提供给我们的最重要的特性：类型。

 function useApi < T > ( url : string ): T | undefined { const [ data , setData ] = useState < T > (); useEffect (() => { fetch ( url ) . then ( r => r . json ()) . then ( json => setData ( json )) }, [ url ]) // Remember your dependencies return data ; }

现在，在我们的组件中，我们将使用正确的类型进行静态验证

export const MyComponent : React . FC = () => { const dogs = useApi < Dog > ( ' /api/v1/dogs ' ); return < DogsList dogs = { dogs } />; } export const MyComponent2 : React . FC = () => { const cats = useApi < Cat > ( ' /api/v1/cats ' ); return < CatsList cats = { cats } />; }

使用第一种方法，我们有：

  • 删除了两个组件中的重复代码
  • 将通信逻辑与组件逻辑分离
  • 为我们的模型添加了静态验证

现在我们对我们的useApi实现感到满意，如果我们正在等待数据，或者在获取我们的资源时出现错误，我们希望向用户显示。

添加一些状态并返回一个元组，我们可以实现以下目标：

 function useApi < T > ( url : string ): [ T | undefined , boolean , Error | undefined ] { const [ data , setData ] = useState < T > (); const [ loading , setLoading ] = useState ( true ); const [ error , setError ] = useState < Error > (); useEffect (() => { setLoading ( true ); fetch ( url ) . then ( r => r . json ()) . then ( json => setData ( json )) . then (() => setLoading ( false )) . catch (( e ) => { setError ( e ); setLoading ( false ); }) }, [ url ]) // Remember your dependencies return [ data , loading , error ]; }

然后在我们的组件中：

 const MyComponent : React . FC = () => { const [ dogs , loading , error ] = useApi ( ' /api/v1/dogs ' ); if ( loading ) { return < div > Loading dogs </ div >; } if ( error ) { return < div > Oops! </ div >; } return < DogsList dogs = { dogs } />; }

结论

通过这种方法，我们能够创建一个自定义挂钩来与 API 进行通信。 我们的组件没有重复的代码，并且我们正在正确处理来自请求的不同状态。

添加另一个库来发出请求很简单，甚至我们可以提取一个fetcher: (url: string) => Promise<T>以允许用户决定使用哪个库。

非常感谢您阅读到最后，希望这对您有所帮助😃。 反馈总是受到赞赏。



原文:

Hi all! This is my first post, and I want to bring an interesting topic:

  • How do we call an API from our react project?
  • What is the best approach?

Of course, there is no silver bullet, and it depends on the project you are working on. Today I will share a few optimizations you can make in your calls and could be a trigger for new ideas.

The problem

During my career, I worked on different projects, and I've found things like this:

Example 1 

export const MyComponent: React.FC = () => { const [dogs, setDogs] = useState(); useEffect(() => { fetch('/api/v1/dogs') .then(r => r.json()) .then(json => setData(json)); }); return <DogsList dogs={dogs} />;
} export const MyComponent2: React.FC = () => { const [cats, setCats] = useState(); useEffect(() => { fetch('/api/v1/cats') .then(r => r.json()) .then(json => setData(json)); }); return <CatsList cats={cats} />;
}

or this:

Example 2 

const MyComponent: React.FC = () => { const [loading, setLoading] = useState(true); const [error, setError] = useState(); const [dogs, setDogs] = useState(); useEffect(() => { fetch('/api/v1/dogs') .then(r => r.json()) .then(json => setDogs(json)) .then(() => setLoading(false)) .catch(e => setError(e)); }); if (loading) { return <div>Loading dogs</div>; } return <DogsList dogs={dogs} />;
}

As you can see, the code starts to duplicate, and we are putting the communication logic inside our component. And it gets even worse if we want to add more logic, i.e. set the state only if the component is mounted.

For some reason, people sometimes forget we can create a simple hook to handle all these scenarios and keep our code cleaner.

Note: Of course, there are libraries to tackle this, but just for the purpose of this article I prefer we implement it by ourselves

1: A simple approach

Let's start with a small implementation of a new hook to retrieve the data from an API. As we are good at naming things here, let's call it useApi:

function useApi(url: string) { const [data, setData] = useState(); useEffect(() => { fetch(url) .then(r => r.json()) .then(json => setData(json)) }, [url]) // Remember your dependencies return data;
}

Only with this simple hook, we could rewrite the first example to this:

export const MyComponent: React.FC = () => { const dogs = useApi('/api/v1/dogs'); return <DogsList dogs={dogs} />;
} export const MyComponent2: React.FC = () => { const cats = useApi('/api/v1/cats'); return <CatsList cats={cats} />;
}

Look how clean this is, my component does not care about how we call this API, if we are using fetch or axios, we just know the data will be there.

A small step for improvement

Let's iterate this a little bit more, we've forgotten some power we have here... We have typescript! And we are not using the most important feature it provides to us: Types.

function useApi<T>(url: string): T | undefined { const [data, setData] = useState<T>(); useEffect(() => { fetch(url) .then(r => r.json()) .then(json => setData(json)) }, [url]) // Remember your dependencies return data;
}

Now, in our components, we are going to have static validation with the proper types

export const MyComponent: React.FC = () => { const dogs = useApi<Dog>('/api/v1/dogs'); return <DogsList dogs={dogs} />;
} export const MyComponent2: React.FC = () => { const cats = useApi<Cat>('/api/v1/cats'); return <CatsList cats={cats} />;
}

With this first approach we have:

  • Removed duplicated code in both components
  • Separated communication logic from component logic
  • Added static validation for our models

2: Managing the state of the request

Now that we are happy with our useApi implementation, we want to display to the user if we are waiting for the data, or if there is an error fetching our resources.

Adding a few states and returning a tuple we can achieve the following:

function useApi<T>(url: string): [T | undefined, boolean, Error | undefined] { const [data, setData] = useState<T>(); const [loading, setLoading] = useState(true); const [error, setError] = useState<Error>(); useEffect(() => { setLoading(true); fetch(url) .then(r => r.json()) .then(json => setData(json)) .then(() => setLoading(false)) .catch((e) => { setError(e); setLoading(false); }) }, [url]) // Remember your dependencies return [data, loading, error];
}

And then in our component:

const MyComponent: React.FC = () => { const [dogs, loading, error] = useApi('/api/v1/dogs'); if (loading) { return <div>Loading dogs</div>; } if (error) { return <div>Oops!</div>; } return <DogsList dogs={dogs} />;
}

Conclusion

With this approach, we were able to create a custom hook to communicate with the API. We don't have repeated code across our components, and we are handling different statuses from the request properly.

Adding another library to make the requests would be straightforward, and even we can extract a fetcher: (url: string) => Promise<T> to allow the users to decide which library to use.

Thank you very much to read until the end, I hope this helped you a little bit 😃. Feedback is always appreciated.
item.author: jor_exe

item.title: 从 HTML 到简单表单：Rails 表单剖析 | From HTML to Simple Form: anatomy of Rails forms
item.description:
在我们的主要项目中，我们喜欢将Simple Form gem用于我们的表单，我们已经使用了将近 10 年，我们仍然欣赏它非常简洁但灵活的语法。

我们也喜欢使用简单表单，我们可以用最少的努力构建外观一致且结构化的表单。 如果您有机会阅读我们之前关于我们使用 View Components 的初步体验的帖子，您可能还记得我们决定在表单中根本不使用 View Components 。 为什么？ 主要是因为Simple Form 已经内置了自己的组件系统，在这篇文章中，我们将简要介绍它在后台是如何工作的。 此外，正如我们将看到的，相同的原则适用于所有 Rails 表单构建器，因此本文中的发现有助于理解 Rails 下构建表单的默认样式。

也就是说，一开始深入了解 Simple Form 表单的细节可能会有些混乱，因为它的语法看起来与默认的 Rails 表单助手有很大不同。 例如，在简单表单中，一行代码可以定义所有内容：表单输入字段、其标签、提示甚至相应的验证错误消息。 那么 Simple Form 是如何真正生成最终的 HTML 标记的呢？

简单表单（和 Rails）表单的剖析

为了证明这一点，让我们尝试一种自下而上的方法。 我们将从纯 HTML 开始，逐步构建相同的表单，使用越来越抽象的语法，直到我们达到简单表单样式。 这将有望帮助我们澄清基本概念。

在最终的 HTML 中，基本表单可能会呈现如下：

 <form action= "/users" method= "post" > <div> <label for= "name" > Enter your name: </label> <input type= "text" name= "name" id= "name" > </div> <div> <label for= "email" > Enter your email: </label> <input type= "email" name= "email" id= "email" > </div> <div> <input type= "submit" value= "Subscribe!" > </div> </form>

该 HTML 可以同样编码在模板中（我们在这里使用Slim 模板语法），如下所示：

 form action = "/users" method = "post" div label > for = "name" Enter your name: input #name type = "text" name = "name" div label > for = "email" Enter your email: input #email type = "text" name = "email" div input type = "submit" value = "Subscribe!"

我们可以使用Rails 表单标签助手获得相同的 HTML 输出（除了可以忽略的细节）。 这里的“标签”一词说明这些是辅助函数，每个函数只能做一件事——渲染特定的 HTML 标签。 事实上，我们只是使用与以前略有不同的语法来呈现我们的表单，而代码结构保持不变：

 = form_tag ( "/users" , method: :post ) div = label_tag :name , "Enter your name: " = text_field_tag :name div = label_tag :email , "Enter your email: " = text_field_tag :email div = submit_tag "Subscribe!"

好的，现在我们来看看更有趣的东西：Rails 表单助手form_with或它的老表亲form_for进行了更大的更改，尤其是当表单绑定到模型对象时，例如@user ：

 = form_with ( model: @user ) do | f | div = f . label :name , "Enter your name: " = f . text_field :name div = f . label :email , "Enter your email: " = f . text_field :email div = f . submit "Subscribe!"

此处使用的form_with表单助手至少执行以下操作：

  • 它检查@user是新记录还是现有记录，并为表单生成正确的路由（操作和方法），
  • 它识别出@user具有例如它的name属性集，并用这个值预填充name字段，
  • 它遵循Rails 约定来命名字段（例如使用name="user[name]"属性），以便提交的 POST 数据可以在目标控制器中很好地处理。

请注意，我们仍然必须自己布局表单：我们定义表单结构，Rails 表单助手负责字段/表单标签。 这种模式是 Rails 中的默认模式，它为我们提供了极大的灵活性——我们可以以任何我们喜欢的方式构建每个表单——但可能会使保持表单一致变得有点困难，尤其是当我们有许多复杂的表单时（这在 web 中很常见） admin 部分），因为我们也必须保持所有表单模板的结构相同。 为了获得更高的一致性，我们可以从 Rails 表单构建器那里获得一些帮助。

轨道形式的建设者

它在表单助手form_with / form_for中实例化，并在表单块中生成。 在上面的代码示例中， f变量是 Rails 表单构建器对象。 构建器方法（ text_field 、 label等）了解表单模型对象（ @user ）并使用表单标签助手来呈现相应的字段、标签或其他元素，这些元素可能被任意 HTML 标签包装。 默认的 Rails 表单构建器只是作为表单标签助手的简单代理，这就是为什么整个表单 HTML 结构默认情况下完全由开发人员负责。

自定义表单生成器

现在，我们可以创建一个自定义表单构建器，例如，收紧呈现的表单 HTML。 最简单的选择是从默认的 Rails 表单构建器中派生它。 在使用表单构建器时，我们需要注意一些内部变量： @template表示我们用来调用底层标签助手方法的视图上下文， @object object 是模型对象 ( @user )， @object_name是它的名字（ "user" ）。

因此，例如，为了支持将文本字段和标签与包装器 div 一起包装的可能性，我们可以定义以下自定义构建器：

 # app/form_builders/labelling_form_builder.rb class LabellingFormBuilder < ActionView :: Helpers :: FormBuilder def text_field ( attribute_name , options = {}) @template . content_tag ( :div , class: "wrapper" ) do label ( attribute_name , options [ :label ]) + super ( attribute_name , options . except ( :label )) end end end

让我们看看这里发生了什么：

  • 我们从默认的 Rails 表单构建器继承自定义构建器类LabellingFormBuilder
  • 我们覆盖其名为text_field的方法，以便它呈现标签和输入字段本身
  • 我们可以通过自定义选项键传递自定义标签文本:label
  • 标签和输入都用包装器 div 包装，注意我们必须使用@template变量作为视图上下文来调用 Rails 视图助手
  • 在模板中，我们使用builder选项指定自定义构建器，并为标签传递自定义选项

好的，我们使用自定义表单构建器成功地修改了生成的字段 HTML 结构，现在我们可以使用一行代码构建一个包含其标签的文本表单字段，并将两个元素都用我们可以设置样式的 div 包裹起来。

另请注意，我们实际上已经创建了“组件”的初始版本来构建表单——表单 HTML 的布局和结构的代码在代码库中的一个地方定义。 我们可以改变这个地方——自定义构建器——它将影响使用该构建器的所有表单。

总体而言，自定义表单构建器可以：

  • 更改输入字段及其随附的 HTML 元素在表单中的呈现和结构方式，
  • 为特殊类型的字段添加方法，
  • 定义一个特定的 API 来构建具有具体结构和布局的表单，从而有助于表单的一致性，
  • 使用所有 ruby​​ 语法围绕我们的表单定义逻辑，定义预设/默认值，样式等。

简单的模式

到目前为止，我们应该有足够的信息来理解 Simple Form gem 的工作原理。 从技术上讲， Simple Form提供了一个自定义的 Rails 表单构建器，但它周围有一组丰富的特性：

  • 使用构建器的辅助方法，尤其是称为input的方法，我们可以生成与表单字段相关的所有内容：字段本身、其标签、提示或错误消息。
  • 或者别的什么，其实！ 它还提供了一组自定义字段，它们具有自己的逻辑，用于处理和渲染模型数据。 值得注意的是，Simple Form 支持对与主模型对象或通用集合相关的记录进行各种自动处理。

使用简单表单，我们的示例表单可以在这样的模板中编码：

 = simple_form_for ( @user ) do | f | = f . input :name , label: "Enter your name" = f . input :email , label: "Enter your email" = f . button :submit , "Subscribe!"

请注意有关表单字段的所有内容是如何在一行中定义的（我们在这里只触及了表面）。 如果我们在构建表单时更喜欢一致性，那么简单表单就很棒。 通过配置简单表单“包装器” ，我们可以定义所有表单的默认结构和布局（当然，如果需要，还可以添加其他表单版本）。 此外，所有布局选项都可以在特定表单或字段中被覆盖，因此如果需要，我们不会失去任何灵活性。

将不同类型的表单助手混合在一起

最后一点，我们可以混合不同层的表单构建助手，甚至在一个表单中。 例如，如果我们尝试使用 Simple Form 呈现表单，但对某些必须特别处理的特定字段使用 Rails 表单标签助手，那么没有什么能阻止我们。

这样做，我们只需要考虑 Rails 的约定，尤其是那些命名表单字段的约定，并在渲染字段时手动遵守它们。 概括

我们遍历了从生成的 HTML 底部到自定义表单构建器和简单表单的所有层。 在以后的文章中，我们将在此信息的基础上描述我们如何创建自定义 Rails 表单构建器，以便方便地使用 Tailwind 为我们的新管理表单设置样式。

如果您不想错过以后的此类帖子，请在此处或Twitter 上关注我。 干杯!



原文:

In our main project, we like to use the Simple Form gem for our forms, we have been using it for nearly 10 years, and we still appreciate its very succinct but flexible syntax.

We also like that using Simple Form, we can build consistently looking and structured forms with minimum effort. If you had a chance to read our previous post about our initial experience with View Components, you may remember that we decided to not use View Components at all for our forms. Why? Mainly because Simple Form has its own component system already built in and in this post we will briefly describe how it works under the hood. Also, as we will see, the same principles are valid for all Rails form builders so findings in this post can be helpful for understanding the default style of building forms under Rails in general.

That said, diving into the details of Simple Form forms may be a bit confusing at first because it’s syntax looks so different from the default Rails form helpers. For example, in Simple Form, a single line of code may define all of: the form input field, its label, a hint or even the corresponding validation error message. So how does Simple Form actually generate the final HTML markup?

Anatomy of a Simple Form (and Rails) form

To show this, let’s try a bottom-up approach. We’ll start with pure HTML and will build gradually, layer by layer, the same form using more and more abstracted syntax until we reach the Simple Form style. This will hopefully help us clarify the underlying concepts.

HTML

A basic form may be rendered like the following in the final HTML:

<form action="/users" method="post"> <div> <label for="name">Enter your name: </label> <input type="text" name="name" id="name"> </div> <div> <label for="email">Enter your email: </label> <input type="email" name="email" id="email"> </div> <div> <input type="submit" value="Subscribe!"> </div>
</form>

This HTML may be equally coded in a template (we use a Slim template syntax here) like this:

form action="/users" method="post" div label> for="name" Enter your name: input#name type="text" name="name" div label> for="email" Enter your email: input#email type="text" name="email" div input type="submit" value="Subscribe!"

Rails form tag helpers

We can get the same HTML output (except negligible details) using the Rails form tag helpers. The word ”tag“ here illustrates that these are helper functions that can each do only one thing – render a particular HTML tag. In fact we are just using a slightly different syntax to render our form than before while the code structure stays the same:

= form_tag("/users", method: :post) div = label_tag :name, "Enter your name: " = text_field_tag :name div = label_tag :email, "Enter your email: " = text_field_tag :email div = submit_tag "Subscribe!"

Rails form helpers

OK, now we’re getting to more interesting stuff: Rails form helpers, form_with or its older cousin form_for, make a bigger change, especially when the form is bound to an model object, such as @user:

= form_with(model: @user) do |f| div = f.label :name, "Enter your name: " = f.text_field :name div = f.label :email, "Enter your email: " = f.text_field :email div = f.submit "Subscribe!"

The form_with form helper used here does at least the following:

  • it checks whether @user is a new or an existing record and generates the proper route (action and method) for the form,
  • it recognizes that the @user has e.g. its name attribute set and prefills the name field with this value,
  • and it follows the Rails conventions to name the fields (e.g. with the name="user[name]" attribute) so that the submitted POST data can be nicely processed in the target controller.

Note that we still have to layout the form by ourselves: we define the form structure and Rails form helpers take care of the field / form tags. This pattern is the default in Rails and gives us great flexibility – we can structure each form any way we like – but perhaps makes it a bit harder to keep the forms consistent, especially when we have many complex forms (as is typical in a web admin section) because we have to keep all the form templates structure the same, too. For greater consistency, we could get some help from Rails form builders.

Rails form builders

A Rails form builder is an object used by Rails to build forms. It is instantiated in the form helper form_with / form_for and is yielded in the form block. In the code sample above, the f variable is a Rails form builder object. The builder methods (text_field, label, etc.) know about the form model object (@user) and use the form tag helpers to render the corresponding fields, labels or other elements, potentially wrapped with arbitrary HTML tags. The default Rails form builder though serves just as a simple proxy to the form tag helpers and that is why the overall form HTML structure is, by default, fully on the developer.

Custom form builder

Now, we can create a custom form builder to, for example, tighten the rendered form HTML. The simplest option is to derive it from the default Rails form builder. There are a few internal variables that we need to be aware of when working with form builders: @template represents the view context that we use to call the underlying tag helper methods and @object is the model object (@user) and @object_name is its name ("user").

So, for example, to support a possibility to wrap a text field and a label together with a wrapper div, we can define the following custom builder:

# app/form_builders/labelling_form_builder.rb
class LabellingFormBuilder < ActionView::Helpers::FormBuilder def text_field(attribute_name, options = {}) @template.content_tag(:div, class: "wrapper") do label(attribute_name, options[:label]) + super(attribute_name, options.except(:label)) end end
end

…and use it in a form template like this:

/ app/views/users/new.html.slim
= form_with(model: @user, builder: LabellingFormBuilder) do |f| = f.text_field :email, label: "Enter your email"

…which generates the following HTML output:

<form action="/users" method="post"> <div class="wrapper"> <label for="user_email">Enter your email</label> <input type="text" name="user[email]" id="user_email" /> </div>
</form>

Let’s see what is going on here:

  • we inherit our custom builder class LabellingFormBuilder from the default Rails form builder
  • we override its method called text_field so that it renders both label and the input field itself
  • we can pass a custom label text via our custom option key :label
  • both label and input are wrapped with a wrapper div, note that we have to use the @template variable the a view context for calling Rails view helpers
  • in the template, we specify our custom builder with the builder option and pass the custom option for the label

OK, we successfully amended the generated fields HTML structure using a custom form builder and now we can build a text form field including its label using a single line of code and have both elements wrapped with a div that we can style.

Also note that we’ve actually created an initial version of a ”component“ to build forms – the code for the layout and structure of the form HTML is defined in a single place in the code base. We can change this one place – the custom builder – and it will affect all forms using that builder.

Overall, custom form builders make it possible to:

  • alter the way input fields and their accompanying HTML elements are rendered and structured in the form,
  • add methods for special types of fields,
  • define a specific API for building forms with a concrete structure and layout, thus helping forms consistency,
  • use all of ruby syntax to define a logic around our forms, define presets / default values, styles and so on.

Simple Form

By now, we should have enough information to understand how the Simple Form gem works. Technically, Simple Form provides a custom Rails form builder but with a rich set of features around it:

  • Using the builder’s helper methods, especially the one called input we can generate everything related to a form field: the field itself, its label, hint or error message.
  • Or anything else, actually! Simple Form makes no assumptions about the form HTML markup and the form structure is fully configurable in a single place – the Simple Form initializer file.
  • It also provides a set of custom fields with their own logic for processing and rendering model data. Notably, Simple Form supports various automatic processing of records associated to the main model object or generic collections.

With Simple Form, our sample form could be encoded in a template like this:

= simple_form_for(@user) do |f| = f.input :name, label: "Enter your name" = f.input :email, label: "Enter your email" = f.button :submit, "Subscribe!"

Note how everything about a form field is defined in a single line (and we only touched the surface here). Simple Form is great if we prefer consistency when building our forms. By configuring Simple Form ”wrappers“, we can define the default structure and layout of all our forms (plus, of course, alternative form versions if we need them). Moreover, all layout options can be overridden in a particular form or field so we don’t lose any flexibility if we need that.

Mixing different types of form helpers together

As a final note, we can mix different layers of form building helpers, even in a single form. For example, nothing would stop us if we tried to render a form using Simple Form but used a Rails form tag helper for some particular field that must be handled specially.

Doing so, we only have to think about Rails conventions, especially those for naming form fields, and obey them manually when rendering the field. The Rails documentation has an example showing how to mix form layers.

Summary

We went through all layers from the bottom of the generated HTML to the custom form builders and Simple Form. In a future post, we will build on this information to describe how we created a custom Rails form builder to conveniently allow styling our new admin forms with Tailwind.

If you don’t want to miss future posts like this, follow me here or on Twitter. Cheers!
